coredata.dc:description: Bilirubin, a crucial component in heme degradation, is formed during the breakdown of red blood cells in the liver and spleen. Elevated bilirubin levels are closely associated with jaundice and serve as markers for various blood and liver-related disorders. Detection of BR has been approached through various methods with fluorescent methods gaining prominence due to their exceptional sensitivity, rapid response, and precision. Among the fluorescence methods, carbon dots have emerged as versatile optical probes, known for their unique optical properties, low toxicity, and ease of synthesis. However, carbon dots typically exhibit a single emission peak, limiting accuracy due to vulnerability to external interferences. To overcome this limitation, probes featuring multiple independent emission signals are preferred. Chlorophyll, abundant in nature and sourced from biomass, is recognized for its safe and fluorescent properties, making it a secure choice for fluorescence-based sensing. This study introduces an innovative approach utilizing Oolong tea dreg as a source of chlorophyll for creating a fluorescent probe to detect bilirubin. Chlorophyll was partially converted into carbon dots using a combination of extraction and hydrothermal techniques, producing a ratiometric probe with two emission signals. The fluorescence of the probe was quenched in the presence of the bilirubin through the inner filter effect. The proposed probe exhibited outstanding selectivity and a low limit of detection (0.8 µM) for bilirubin. Furthermore, the probe demonstrated satisfactory recovery rates when tested with artificial urine and human urine samples.

head.ce:title: Innovative fluorescent ratiometric probe from Oolong tea dreg for highly sensitive bilirubin detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bilirubin, a crucial component in heme degradation, is formed during the breakdown of red blood cells in the liver and spleen. Elevated bilirubin levels are closely associated with jaundice and serve as markers for various blood and liver-related disorders. Detection of BR has been approached through various methods with fluorescent methods gaining prominence due to their exceptional sensitivity, rapid response, and precision. Among the fluorescence methods, carbon dots have emerged as versatile optical probes, known for their unique optical properties, low toxicity, and ease of synthesis. However, carbon dots typically exhibit a single emission peak, limiting accuracy due to vulnerability to external interferences. To overcome this limitation, probes featuring multiple independent emission signals are preferred. Chlorophyll, abundant in nature and sourced from biomass, is recognized for its safe and fluorescent properties, making it a secure choice for fluorescence-based sensing. This study introduces an innovative approach utilizing Oolong tea dreg as a source of chlorophyll for creating a fluorescent probe to detect bilirubin. Chlorophyll was partially converted into carbon dots using a combination of extraction and hydrothermal techniques, producing a ratiometric probe with two emission signals. The fluorescence of the probe was quenched in the presence of the bilirubin through the inner filter effect. The proposed probe exhibited outstanding selectivity and a low limit of detection (0.8 µM) for bilirubin. Furthermore, the probe demonstrated satisfactory recovery rates when tested with artificial urine and human urine samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bilirubin (BR) is a yellow compound that plays a crucial role in the breakdown of heme, a component of hemoglobin in red blood cells. It is created in the liver and spleen during the decomposition of red blood cells. The liver subsequently processes the bilirubin, which is ultimately eliminated from the body through bile. It is well-known for its connection to jaundice, a disorder marked by a yellowing of the skin and eyes that happens when bilirubin levels rise. Bilirubin is an important part of clinical diagnostics because it also serves as a marker for a number of blood and liver-related illnesses [1,2]. It is present in two forms: unconjugated BR and conjugated BR. Bilirubin is often found in plasma primarily originates from the breakdown of red blood cells. This process occurs within macrophages, where heme is converted into biliverdin via heme oxygenase and further transformed into bilirubin through biliverdin reductase. Once formed, bilirubin enters the plasma and is transported to the liver bound to albumin due to its insolubility in water. Despite this binding, it's referred to as unconjugated bilirubin. Within the liver, hepatocytes absorb unconjugated bilirubin and facilitate its conjugation with glucuronic acid, a reaction catalyzed by the enzyme uridine diphosphate–glucuronyl transferase. This conjugation renders bilirubin soluble in water, hence termed conjugated bilirubin. When the liver or bile ducts has problems, where bilirubin is not properly processed and ends up in the bloodstream, leading to its excretion through the kidneys into urine [3]. The typical serum bilirubin level ranges from 0.3 to 1.9 mg/dL. Lower concentrations of bilirubin in the serum have been associated with conditions such as coronary heart disease and iron deficiency. Conversely, when the bilirubin concentration exceeds 2.5 mg/dL, it can lead to hyperbilirubinemia, causing metabolic disorders [4,5]. Evaluating bilirubin levels in urine provides significant insights into liver function and the presence of particular medical conditions like liver disease, hemolytic anemia, or obstructive jaundice. Jaundice, characterized by yellowing of the skin and eyes as bilirubin levels rise, is predominantly linked with bilirubin.

Various methods for bilirubin detection have been employed [4], including high-performance liquid chromatography [6,7], capillary electrophoresis [8], electrochemical [9,10], and optical and chemical approaches such as spectroscopic, chemiluminescence, and fluorometric methods [11–15]. In contrast to alternative techniques, fluorescent (FL) methods offer notable advantages, such as exceptional sensitivity, rapid response, and precise accuracy. Furthermore, they are relatively straightforward and do not necessitate complex systems. Several FL probes, including materials like metal nanoclusters [16,17], quantum dots [18,19], metal–organic framework [20], carbon dots [21,22], organic fluorescence [23,24] have been proposed.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable interest as optical probes due to their distinctive optical characteristics, minimal toxicity, and straightforward synthesis. The optical attributes of carbon dots, including customizable emission, exceptional resistance to photodegradation, and adaptability, make them versatile instruments suitable for an extensive array of applications [25–29]. Numerous methods have been developed to synthesize CDs, such as laser ablation of polymers [30], chemical vapor deposition using C2H2 as a carbon source [31], microwave heating of citric acid [32], ultrasonic treatment of polyamide resin [33]. However, most of these techniques involve intricate processes and costly chemical precursors. Therefore, a straightforward synthesis approach utilizing biomass sources as both carbon and nitrogen sources become imperative. Typically, carbon dots exhibit a single emission peak. However, single-emission fluorescence probes have certain limitations, as their emission signals often lack the required accuracy due to susceptibility to external interferences. Therefore, there is a preference for probes featuring two or more independent emission signals [34].

Chlorophyll (CHL), abundant in nature and obtainable from biomass sources like eubacteria and plants, is known for its distinctive porphyrin structure, imparting outstanding fluorescence properties. It is considered a trustworthy and secure choice for applications in fluorescence-based sensing [35–41]. Plants featuring deep green leaves typically contain abundant chlorophyll, as seen in celery, cilantro, collard greens, tea, mustard greens, spinach, and wheatgrass [42].

Tea plays a role in various cultural customs. Most of tea’s positive health effects can be attributed to the antioxidant activity of its polyphenolic components. This has made tea a popular beverage worldwide. Although main component of tea is a group of polyphenolic compounds, polyphenols and CHL are related to quality of tea. CHL content in fresh tea leaf varies from 1.39 to 5.39 mg/g [43–45]. As tea is widely consumed, our study focused on Oolong tea dregs, which are always discarded after tea beverage preparation. These tea dregs served as a precursor for preparing a fluorescent BR probe. To the best of our knowledge, this work reports for the first time the use of tea dreg as source of fluorescent material. The BR probe was developed through the partial transformation of CHL into CDs using a hydrothermal technique after extracting CHL from the Oolong tea dreg. The collected probe consists of two emission peaks across a wide excitation range. The fluorescence intensity of each peak was decreased at different rates by the presence of the BR. Utilizing the fluorescence ratiometric technique, our suggested probe with two emission signals demonstrated high selectivity and a low limit of detection for BR. Furthermore, to confirm the efficacy of the proposed probe, an artificial urine sample was employed for validation purposes.

Oolong tea was acquired from Lamdong, Vietnam. Bilirubin was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (B4126, Sigma-Aldrich, USA). The bilirubin used in this study contains three different isomers, whose main component is alpha-isomers (unconjugated BR), with trace amount of beta (monoconjugated BR) and gamma-isomers (bisconjugated BR). Amino acids, such as alanine, arginine, aspartic acid, asparagine, cysteine, glutamine, glutamic acid, histidine, leucine, lysine, phenylalanine, proline, tryptophan, tyrosine, and valine, were purchased from Tokyo Chemial Industry (TCI, Japan). Ethanol (EtOH), acetone, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Samchun, Korea. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ·cm was used.

UV–visible absorption spectra were measured using a UV–visible/NIR spectrophotometer (V-770, Jasco, Japan). The FL properties of the prepared materials were recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). A fluorescence lifetime investigation was conducted with a Fluorolog QM-75-22-C (Horiba, Canada). For excitation, a pulsed diode laser with single-mode emission at 375 nm, a pulse width of approximately 45 ps, and an average power of approximately 1.8 mW was employed. Emission data were acquired using a time-correlated single photon counting system (DeltaHub™ DH-HT High throughput TSCPC controller, Horiba, Canada). Functional groups in the prepared samples were identified using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) with a Jasco FR/IR-4600 spectrometer in attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. Zeta potentials of the samples were recorded using a dynamic light scattering instrument with a unique Ω-shaped capillary at 25 °C in water (Litesizer DLS 500, Anton Paar, USA). To determine the valence states and chemical composition of the samples, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed utilizing an Axis Ultra system (Kratos Analytical Ltd.) equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of chlorophyll- carbon dots material
ce:section.ce:para: After using 20 g of oolong tea to prepare a soft drink, the tea dreg was collected. To maximize the removal of tannins from the tea dreg, it was soaked for 30 min at room temperature, followed by rapid heating to 80 °C with stirring for 30 min, and then filtration. Subsequently, water was added and heated at 80 °C for 20 min, and then filtered [46]. This procedure was repeated three times. The treated tea dreg was air-dried and the dried material subsequently was finely chopped into small pieces. For CHL extraction, two grams of the dried tea dreg were soaked in 100 mL of ethanol, vigorously stirred for 12 h, and then the mixture was subjected to centrifugation and filtration to remove any undissolved residues. The chlorophyll extracted from the tea dreg was stored as a stock solution at 4 °C. To produce the BR probe, a CHL solution was heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to collect the supernatant solution, which was subsequently employed as a BR probe. The probe was stored in a refrigerator.

Various amounts of the BR solution were introduced into 0.5 mL of the BR probe solution, and phosphate buffer with a pH of 7 was added to reach a total volume of 1.5 mL. The mixture was agitated for 3 min. Subsequently, the FL spectrum was captured using an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The FL intensities of the peaks, arising from CDs and CHL, were measured based on the areas under the emission curves.

To verify its availability, the developed method was applied to determine BR in artificial urine samples. In accordance with the earlier reports [47,48], a urine stock sample was formulated. In short, 170 mg of Na2SO4, 25 mg of uric acid, 88 mg of creatinine, 1.5 g of urea, 230 mg of KCl, 176 mg of NaCl, 18.5 mg of CaCl2, 127 mg of NH4Cl, 3.5 mg of K2C2O4·H2O, 108 mg of MgSO4·7H2O, 83 mg of Na2HPO4·2H2O, and 291 mg of NaH2PO4·2H2O were incorporated into 100 mL of distilled water. Furthermore, human urine samples from three healthy volunteers from our school were used to assess the method. To avoid the interference from sample matrix as much as possible, the urine sample was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min), and the supernatant was diluted 50 times [49,50]. The spike method was used to detect BR in the artificial urine sample and human urine sample. Each spiked concentration underwent four independent measurements.

All experimental procedures were performed in compliance with the relevant laws, regulations and institutional guidelines, and the use of human-derived samples in this study was approved by the Public Institutional Review Board Designated by Ministry of Health and Welfare (P01-202409–02-003). Informed consents were obtained from the participants for the publication of any images and other data included in the manuscript. No violation of human rights occurred during this investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the BR probe and its optical properties
Effect of solvent for extraction of CHL
ce:section.ce:para: During the last decade, several methods have been suggested for extracting CHL. The efficiency of extraction, content of CHL a and CHL b, and optical properties of the extracted solution depend on the solvent, method, and material. The solvents most commonly used have been acetone, DMF, DMSO, EtOH, and methanol [51–58]. The purpose of our study was to develop a fluorescent probe from CHL using simple procedures; therefore, we did not focus on developing an extraction procedure for obtaining pure CHL from the tea dreg. The study investigated the impact of various solvents on the extraction of chlorophyll from oolong tea dreg. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the fluorescence excitation spectrum broadened towards shorter wavelengths when the solvents DMF, acetone, and ethanol were used in that order. The blue shift of the excitation spectra could be related to the solvent’s polarity. The dielectric constants of DMF, ethanol, and acetone are 38.3, 24.3, and 20.7, respectively. Low polarity results in hypsochromic shifts (blue shift) of the absorption spectra, consequently leading to the blue shift of the excitation spectra. Despite ethanol having a higher polarity than acetone, the excitation spectrum of CHL broadened into the blue range when using ethanol. This phenomenon may be attributed to the extraction efficiency, where chlorophyll a and b coexist [59,60].

The variations in the chlorophyll concentration (measured through fluorescence intensity) over extraction time using different solvents are shown in Fig. 1b. Notably, the extraction performance saturates at distinct time intervals: 4 h for acetone, 10 h for ethanol, and 11 h for DMF. Subsequently, ethanol was chosen for further research to enhance its potential for future applications, given its convenience in storage. The decision to choose ethanol was influenced by its boiling points 78 °C, which more favorable for the intended applications compared to the boiling points of acetone (56 °C), and DMF (153 °C). Furthermore, it is noteworthy that DMF is considered more toxic than ethanol.

Chlorophyll is typically not soluble in water. Nevertheless, we are interested in understanding the effects when diluted ethanol is employed to extract CHL from tea dregs. The effect of ethanol/water ratio used in the extraction process on the FL properties was studied at three ratios (10:0, 8:2, and 6:4). Fig. 2 a presents the 3-dimensional (3D) FL spectra and contour plots of the samples. As the ethanol/water ratios changed from 6:4 to 10:0, the excitation range expanded towards shorter wavelengths, and the emission peak shifted slightly towards longer wavelengths. The broadening of the excitation spectrum, associated with the ethanol content in the extraction solvent, may stem from the aggregation or formation of dimers/higher-order complexes, leading to alterations in energy levels. The emission spectrum of the extracted CHL solution features a prominent peak at 670 nm, along with a shoulder at 720 nm, corresponding to the emission from chlorophyll [41].

Fig. 2b displays the excitation spectra of the CHL samples. As the ethanol concentration increased, the excitation spectrum shifted towards shorter wavelengths and broadened. The reason can be attributed to the existence of chlorophyll monomer, dimer, and oligomers [61,62]. While the emission peak of the CHL solution changed insignificantly in all cases, the excitation spectrum of the 10:0 ratio sample was broader than that of others, providing flexibility in choosing the light source for excitation.

Meanwhile, Fig. 2c presents the absorption spectra of the samples. Notably, in the case of samples with ratios of 8:2 and 6:4, the peaks at 411 nm and 667 nm, corresponding to the Soret band and Q-band features of chlorophyll porphyrins [63,64], showed a decrease in intensity compared to the sample with a 10:0 ratio This phenomenon may be attributed to the efficiency of chlorophyll extraction by ethanol. Conversely, the increase in the peak at 274 nm may be attributed to the presence of other organic compounds co-extracted by water. As a result, 10:0 ethanol/water ratio was selected for further research. Fig. 2d displays the images of the samples extracted using pure EtOH, whose photos were taken under the ambient light and UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on the transformation of CHL into CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In anticipation of synthesizing CDs from the CHL solution, the extracted solution was subjected to varying temperatures for duration of 10 h. Contour plots (Fig. S1a) shows that a peak at 450 nm, attribute to CDs, emerged when the temperature exceeded 130 °C. Furthermore, at 190 °C, the intensity of the peak associated with CDs surpassed that of the peak associated with CHL. To determine the remaining CHL concentrations in the solutions, the absorption spectra of the samples were measured, as shown in Fig. S1b. Fig. S1c shows the calculation of remaining CHL concentrations in the solutions. The solution were prepared by extracting two grams of tea dreg in 100 mL of ethanol, and the calculations were performed using the Beer-Lambert law with an extinction efficiency of 74.5 mL/mg·cm [65], as shown in Fig. S1c. The depletion of CHL concentration resulted from the transformation of CHL into CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of heating duration on the optical characteristics of the probe prepared at 160 °C
ce:section.ce:para: The sample subjected to a heating temperature of 160 °C was chosen for optimizing the heating time. In Fig. S2a, the contour plots illustrate the excitation-emission profiles alongside FL intensity for samples with varying heating times. The presence of a peak corresponding to CDs was only observed when the reaction time exceeded 4 h. The emission spectrum of the sample treated for more than 6 h consisted of two peaks – one in the UV range related to CD emission and the other in the near IR range related to CHL emission. The FL emission peak of CHL shifted to a shorter wavelength as the heating time increased. This shift could be attributed to the conversion of chlorophyll a to chlorophyll b [37,66]. Fig. S2b presents the FL intensity of CD and CHL-related peaks in the samples treated for 6 h under various excitation wavelengths. The FL intensity of the CDs decreased as excitation wavelength increased from 365 to 410 nm, but it increased with longer reaction times. In contrast, the FL intensity of the CHL increased with longer excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence intensity of the samples remained unchanged during two months, demonstrating the high fluorescence stability of the BR probes.

Fig. 3 a shows the absorption spectra of the as-extracted CHL sample and samples subjected to varying heating durations, ranging from 2 to 10 h. The peaks at 411 nm and 667 nm exhibited insignificant changes when the samples were treated at 160 °C for less than 6 h. However, these peaks exhibited a substantial decrease when the heating time exceeded 6 h, indicating the transformation of CHL into CDs. Fig. 3b presents the calculated CHL concentrations in the solutions. The samples treated at 160 °C for 6 h and 8 h were assigned labels E160-6 and E160-8, respectively. Fig. 3c displays the images of the E160-6 sample under sunlight and UV light, showcasing its optical characteristics.

The FTIR spectrum of the E160-6 sample is shown in Fig. 3d. Prominent absorption bands between 3200 and 3700 cm−1 are evident, signifying the presence of N–H and O–H stretching vibrations. The two strong absorption bands observed at 2926 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1 are attributed to CH2 and C–H stretching vibrations. The peak at 1757 cm−1 and 1626 cm−1 correspond to the stretching modes of carbonyl groups C=O in a conjugated system and C=C, respectively. The peak at 1285 cm−1 is likely associated with C–N stretching, while the peak at 1065 cm−1 may originated from C–O/ C–N stretching vibrations. Peaks in the range of 850–650 cm−1 could be related with in-plane and out-of-plane bending vibrations of the porphin rings [41,67,68]. These finding suggest that the E160-6 surface existed nitrogen and oxygen containing functional groups, contributing to the water solubility of the sample.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is a widely employed technique for characterizing materials, offering the capability to determine the binding energy of a core-level electron in a solid-state atom. The analyst of the E160-6 sample using XPS (in Fig. S3a) revealed the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, aligning with expectation and consistent with finding from previous study [41]. The absence of Mg2+ ion in the XPS scans could be attributed to either an insufficient amount of Mg2+ or potential loss during hydrothermal process. In Fig. S3b, the deconvoluted C1s spectrum shows three distinct peaks at 288.6 eV, 285.7 eV, and 284.5 eV, corresponding to C=O, C–O/C–N, C=C, respectively. The broader O1s peak suggests the existence of various oxygen species. Further examination of the O1s peak fitting reveals contributions from C–O–H, C=O, C–O, and O–O species, corresponding to binding energies of 532.7 eV, 531.8 eV, 531.4 eV, and 530.3 eV, respectively (Fig. S3c). Regarding N1s, although deconvolution of the spectrum was not feasible, two discernible peaks at 399.6 eV and 401.9 eV were observed, possibly associated with C–N and N–O vibrations (Fig. S3d).

To achieve the highest sensitivity, we investigated the response of E160-6 and E160-8 probes to varying BR concentrations under different excitations. As shown in Figs. S4 and S5, in all cases, upon the addition of BR, the FL intensities of CDs and the CHL peak gradually decreased, albeit at different levels. However, we observed that the ICHL/ICD ratio changed linearly with respect to the BR concentration in the range of 1–50 µM. The slopes of the fitting curves increased with the excitation wavelength, and the coefficient of determination (R2) reached 0.99 when the samples were excited with a 380 nm wavelength. The reason could be attributed to the excitation wavelength-dependent emission efficiency of CDs and CHL. This will be elucidated further in the mechanism section through the overlap between the excitation spectra of the probe and the absorption spectrum of the BR.

Fig. 4 a displays the fluorescence spectra of the E160-6 probe in response to various concentrations of BR under excitation at a 380 nm wavelength. The fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased more rapidly than that of CHL, as shown in Fig. 4b. As depicted in Fig. 4c, within the range of 1 µM to 50 µM, the relationship between BR concentration and ICHL/ICDs is expressed by the following equation: ICHL/ICDs = 26.06 [BR] + 0.913, with an R2 value of 0.99. Here, [BR] represents the BR concentration in mM, while ICHL and ICDs correspond to the fluorescence intensities of CHL and CDs, respectively. The calculated limit of detection, determined to be 0.8 µM, is comparable to values reported for other fluorescence probes. A comparison of the suggested approach is summarized in Table 1 .

We investigated how changes in pH affected the FL intensity of the E160-6 probe. The results in Fig. S6 show that the FL intensities of both CDs and CHL in the probe were not significantly affected by pH fluctuations within the range of 5 to 7.

To elucidate the FL quenching mechanism of BR, the excitation spectra of E160-6 probe and UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the BR were measured. The emission spectrum of E160-6 probe consists of two peaks: one originated from the CDs, and the other from CHL component. Therefore, we recorded excitation spectra of these peaks. As shows in Fig. 5 a, the excitation spectrum of CD emission reached its maximum at 360 nm and cut off at 400 nm. Meanwhile, the excitation spectrum of the CHL peak exhibited a broader range from 360 nm to 420 nm, cutting off at 450 nm, with its intensity being higher than that of CD. The absorption spectrum of the BR fully overlaps with the excitation spectrum of the CD peak and partially overlaps with the excitation spectrum of the CHL peak. Furthermore, the absorption spectrum of the E160-6 probe, when BR was present, resembles the sum of the individual absorption spectra of BR and the E160-6 probe (Fig. 5b). This suggests that the addition of BR to the E160-6 probe did not result in the formation of a new complex, or that it could lead to the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between BR probe and BR. Additionally, we measured the zeta potentials of the E160-6 probes both with and without the addition of BR (50 µM). The results showed an insignificant change in the mean zeta potential of the probe, going from −44.3855 mV to −44.3436 mV, as shown in Fig. S7. This suggests a potential adsorption interaction between the E160-6 probe and BR molecules. Based on these results, the possible mechanisms could include inner filter effect and Förster resonance energy transfer.

The FL lifetime of the E160-6 probe before and after adding BR was also examined. Fig. 5c and d display fluorescence decay curves for CDs and CHL emission peaks of the E160-6 probe when excited at 375 nm. Analyzing using a bi-exponential function revealed average lifetimes of τ = 3.45 ns and τ = 3.43 ns for the CD-related emission peak with and without BR, respectively. Similarly, the CHL-related emission peak displayed average lifetimes of τ = 6.50 ns and τ = 6.49 ns, with and without BR, respectively. The steady stability of the emission peak lifetimes suggests that there was no electron transfer process between them and that BR had no effect on the structural integrity of the E160-6 probe.

In summary, the overlap between the absorption spectrum of BR and the excitation spectra of the E160-6 probe, the fact that the probe’s absorption spectrum remains unchanged upon BR addition, and the insignificant alternation in the lifetime of the probe’s excited state regardless of BR addition suggested that static quenching with an inner filter effect is the primary mechanism of FL quenching [79,80].

The E160-6 probe shows no significant interference from compounds such as phenylalanine, tryptophan, valine, proline, leucine, histidine, glutamine, glutamic acid, cysteine, aspartic acid, asparagine, ascorbic acid, alanine, arginine (5 mM), which are expected to be present in real sample, as demonstrated in Fig. S8a. The selectivity of the prepared E160-6 probe was also assessed in the presence of various similar compounds, including glutathione, glucose, bisphenol A, urea, uric acid, and metal ions. The results showed that the emission intensities of CDs and CHL remained largely unchanged in the presence of 10 µM of BR and 5 mM of other compounds (Fig. S8b). These results demonstrate the high selectivity of the E160-6 probe toward BR.

Bilirubin typically doesn't show up in urine in notable quantities in healthy individuals. It's a byproduct of red blood cell breakdown, mostly eliminated through the liver into bile, then excreted in feces. However, in specific situations, bilirubin may be detected in urine. This usually signals liver or bile duct issues, where bilirubin isn't properly processed and enters the bloodstream, eventually reaching the kidneys and being expelled in urine. Presence of bilirubin in urine often indicates liver ailments like hepatitis, cirrhosis, bile duct blockages (like gallstones), or other conditions impacting bilirubin metabolism. Therefore; artificial and human urine were used as the actual sample to assess the viability and precision of the proposed method through spike recovery analysis. Table 2 represent the results of fluorescence spectrophotometry for detecting BR in artificial and human urine samples. The recovery of BR in the samples ranged from 97.2 % to 103.5 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.0 % to 3.8 %. These data support the practical effectiveness of the developed method in real applications.

The BR probe was fabricated from oolong tea dregs using a straightforward green method without the addition of any chemical compounds. Chlorophyll was extracted from oolong tea dreg with EtOH and partially transformed into carbon dots through a simple heating process. The operating principle of the probe relies on the fluorescence ratiometric technique, where the BR quenches two emissions of the probe at different rates due to the inner filter effect. While this study does not significantly improve upon previous methods in terms of the linear range and LOD, its notable strengths lie in its eco-friendly nature and flexible excitation wavelength within the range of 350–400 nm. The results shown in this study provides a promising and environment-friendly method for ratiometric probe synthesis, which can be used for sensitive detection of biomarkers and potentially applicable for health monitoring and disease diagnosis.

Joong Ho Shin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Data curation.
